title: The Healer's Round
desc: A healer is making rounds. She could use an extra pair of hands.

steps.text:
The healer moves through the city like water — flowing from one patient to the next without pausing. She carries a leather satchel that jingles with bottles, a belt hung with dried herbs, and an expression of determined calm.

"Twelve stops today," she tells you, already walking. "Three post-combat recoveries, two chronic conditions, four seasonal ailments, two injuries from someone who tried to ride a creature that didn't want riding, and one who ate something from the Forest of Life without asking what it was first."

She glances at you. "You carry the satchel. I'll do the healing. Don't open any bottle with a red cap unless I say so."

options.label: Carry the satchel and keep up

steps.text:
"Concentrated healing tincture. A drop cures a wound. A splash knocks someone unconscious. A full dose and they sleep for three days." She says this with the casual tone of someone who's measured the difference.

The rounds are fascinating. A warrior with a shoulder that won't rotate — she sings a two-note Mugic hum while massaging it, and the joint pops back into place. An elderly creature whose vision is fading — she applies a paste of ground crystal to his temples and his eyes brighten.

At the eleventh stop, she asks for the red-cap bottle. You hand it over carefully. She applies a single drop to a deep wound that hasn't healed in weeks. The wound seals itself, skin knitting together in real time.

"One drop," she says, capping the bottle. "Remember that."

Twelfth stop: the creature who ate the Forest of Life fruit. He's turned slightly green and is growing moss on his left arm. She sighs. "This'll take a while."

You hoist the satchel — it's heavier than it looks — and follow. She walks fast. You walk faster.

The rounds blur together. Each patient is different, each treatment precise. She knows every name, every history, every allergy. She mixes remedies on the spot, grinding herbs on a portable stone, humming Mugic under her breath.

At the seventh stop, she asks you to hold a patient's hand while she sets a bone. The patient — a young warrior who tried to ride an untamed creature and was thrown into a wall — squeezes your hand hard enough to leave marks.

"Thank you," the warrior says afterward. Not to the healer. To you. "The hand-holding. It helps."

The healer catches your eye and nods. "That's the part they don't teach. Sometimes the most important medicine is someone being there."

options.label: Carry the satchel through the last five stops
